pattern: A square field only needs one horizontal gap and one vertical gap of matching width, so collecting every possible gap width along each axis (including the two fixed border fences) and intersecting the two collections finds the biggest square directly, without ever choosing which individual fences to remove.
hints: Every horizontal fence position, together with the two border fences that can never be removed, defines a set of possible vertical gap widths just by taking every pairwise difference between two of those positions.
Build that same kind of set for the vertical fence positions and their horizontal gap widths, then the side length of any achievable square must appear in both sets at once.
The answer is the largest value common to both difference sets, squared and reduced modulo one billion and seven; if the two sets share nothing, no square field is possible.

edge_inputs.description: a small field where one removable fence on each axis lines up to a square
a small field where no combination of removable fences can ever line up
the smallest allowed field dimensions with a single interior fence on each axis
several candidate gap widths exist on each axis, so the answer must be the largest shared one, not the first one found
field dimensions and fence positions near the maximum coordinate, stressing large-square modular arithmetic
one axis is tiny and the other is enormous, so their gap widths never overlap
removing every interior fence on both axes still leaves a square exactly as large as the whole field
the two axes have the same number of interior fences but at different offsets, so only some gap widths coincide

stress.description: random large field dimensions with the maximum number of randomly placed interior fences on each axis
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(3, 10**9)
    n = rng.randint(3, 10**9)
    k = max(1, min(size, 600))
    hCount = min(k, max(1, m - 2))
    vCount = min(k, max(1, n - 2))
    hFences = rng.sample(range(2, m), hCount) if m > 2 else [2]
    vFences = rng.sample(range(2, n), vCount) if n > 2 else [2]
    return [m, n, sorted(hFences), sorted(vFences)]

complexity.time: O((h+v)^2) where h and v are the fence counts
